main_sections.title: Three Days to See
main_sections.prose_summary: This essay is an adapted extract from Helen Keller's celebrated personal essay in which she, a blind and deaf author, reflects on what she would most like to see if given sight for just three days. The piece opens with Keller describing the rich sensory world she accesses through touch alone — the delicate symmetry of a leaf, the smooth bark of a silver birch versus the shaggy bark of a pine, the quiver of a singing bird felt through a small tree. Yet touch intensifies rather than satisfies her longing: if so much beauty is available without sight, how much more must vision reveal? She imagines dividing three days of sight into three parts. On the first day she would see the people whose kindness and companionship have made her life worth living; unable to look into friends' eyes, she currently knows them only by the feel of their faces and can detect broad emotions but not the subtleties the eye communicates. On the second day she would arise with the dawn, witness the miracle of night turning to day, and then devote the hours to a hasty glimpse of the world's history — visiting museums to see the condensed pageant of humankind's progress, including the remains of prehistoric creatures. On the third day she would return to the living world — standing at a busy city corner merely watching people going about their lives, reading smiles and suffering and determination on their faces. At midnight sight would close again. She closes with a passionate appeal to sighted readers: use your eyes as if tomorrow you might be struck blind; apply the same urgency to hearing, touch, smell, and taste. Of all the senses, she concludes, sight must be the most delightful. The essay's tone moves from quiet wonder and poignant longing to inspiring advocacy for mindful, grateful engagement with the world.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details of that place and write about the following: 1. Name of the place  2. The person(s) you went with  3. The things that you could: see / listen / smell / taste / touch and feel

question_bank.stem: Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details and write: 1. Name of the place  2. The person(s) you went with  3. The things that you could: see / listen / smell / taste / touch and feel

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. II How does this memory make you feel? III Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear? Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: How does this memory make you feel?
Do you think your visit/experience would be different if you could not see or hear?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Work in pairs. Identify the statements that are true from the ones given below. Share your answers with the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Identify the statements that are true: 1. The author expresses a deep longing to experience the world through the sense of hearing. 2. The author would like exploring the city only to observe how people are suffering. 3. The author would spend the first day seeing the people whose kind-heartedness and friendship have enriched her life. 4. The author acknowledges that even in three days, she would not be able to see everything. 5. The author feels that everyone can find the greatest joy merely by the sense of touch. 6. The author advises those who can see to appreciate every moment as if they might lose their senses tomorrow. 7. On the third day, the author would like to observe people in their daily lives and understand their experiences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Complete the table given below with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with what Helen Keller wanted to do on the three days if she had sight.
question_bank.table:
Day 1|Day 2|Day 3
see the people, know her friends from the feel of their faces, …|arise with the dawn, …|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — III. The author says that you need eyes to be able to 'see' the true self of a person. Therefore, she refers to the eye as 'window of the soul'. How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?

question_bank.stem: How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 1: 'I feel the delicate symmetry of a leaf...feel the happy quiver of a bird in full song.' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: What does the 'delicate symmetry of a leaf' symbolise?
List two phrases from the extract that describe the texture of objects in nature.
Complete the sentence appropriately. The phrase 'awakening Nature after her winter's sleep' refers to __________________.
What is the tone of the writer in this extract? A. nostalgic  B. proud  C. admiring  D. celebratory

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Extract 2: 'The next day I should arise with the dawn...and so I should go to the museums.' Answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: The word 'thrilling' has been used with 'miracle'. Which of the following words does not match with the word 'thrilling'? A. experience  B. tale  C. knowledge  D. adventure
Why does the writer refer to the earth as 'sleeping earth'?
Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. The writer wishes to dedicate the day to a brief look at the present and past world because __________________.
Why does the writer use 'should' multiple times in the extract?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: The sense of touch makes up for the loss of sight and hearing. Explain this statement with reference to the text.
Why does the author believe that the sense of sight is the most wonderful?
How might the author's opinion on making the most of our senses guide us to be kinder towards people with special abilities?
What is the significance of imagining the loss of a sense, according to the author?
How does the author encourage people to approach their everyday sensory experiences?
What do the author's choices for the three days tell us about her values and priorities?

listening.transcript_text:
Anuj: Ma, we have a new student, Tara. She is talkative and pleasant. The writing in her books looks different — she said it is called Braille.
Mother: It is great that your school has facilities for the visually impaired. Thanks to the Digital India Initiative, which is paving the way for inclusion.
Anuj: Digital India — that is about being computer literate, isn't it?
Mother: It is more than internet access. It aims to include everyone, including people with disabilities, through digital infrastructure and services. For the visually impaired it means assistive technologies like screen readers, voice-over-enabled smartphones and apps.
Anuj: What is a screen reader, Ma?
Mother: A screen reader is software that helps visually impaired people use computers and smartphones. It reads out the text on the screen and provides audio feedback.
Anuj: So Tara can use a computer just like we do?
Mother: Yes. It ensures visually impaired people can use digital services independently, improving their quality of life and opening job opportunities in fields like IT and banking.
Anuj: So they can work and do things on their own? I am proud of my school.
Mother: Yes. Remember, inclusion is not just about having the right tools, but also the right mindset.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and son. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: Select four true statements: 1. Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more about it. 2. Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment. 3. Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers. 4. Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired. 5. Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones. 6. Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies for persons who are visually impaired. 7. Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most. Use the given prompts to frame your response before you speak.

question_bank.stem: Speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most using the prompts: I value my sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste the most because… / One of my favourite experiences is… / I appreciate being able to see/ hear/touch/ smell/ taste… / God forbid, if I were to lose the sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste, it would make me feel… / I would not be able to… / I thank God that I am able to see/ hear/ touch/ smell / taste.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A descriptive paragraph describes a person, place, object or event to create a memorable experience for the reader. Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently. Follow the guidelines given below: 1. In the opening line, begin with something interesting to capture the reader's attention. Introduce the place you visited. 2. In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and specific details that appealed to your five senses—sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. You may include alliterations, similes, and personifications. 3. In the concluding lines, summarise the description to provide a final impression.

question_bank.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently following the three-part structure: (1) opening line to capture attention, (2) supporting lines using sensory details — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — with alliterations, similes and personifications, (3) concluding lines with a final impression.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Sensory words are descriptive — they describe how we experience the world. Complete the following table with sensory words from the text in Column 2 for the five senses in Column 1. Add new words in Column 3.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table with sensory words from the text and add new words in Column 3.
question_bank.table:
Column 1 (Sense)|Column 2 (From text)|Column 3 (New words)
What you see|panorama|
What you hear|music|
What you smell|perfume|
What you taste|relish|
What you feel|rough|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Read the following sentences from the text and underline the verbs. Modal verbs are auxiliary verbs that express necessity, possibility, permission, or ability. III Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences. Choose the functions they express from those given in the box: possibility  necessity  polite request  ability  moral obligation

question_bank.stem: Identify the modal verbs in the following sentences and choose the functions they express from the box (possibility / necessity / polite request / ability / moral obligation): 1. You can learn a lot from this experience. 2. They might arrive late due to traffic. 3. She must finish her homework before dinner. 4. They ought to apologise for their mistake. 5. He would like to have some lassi, please.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV Read the situations in Column 1 and functions in Column 2. Fill in the blanks in the sentences in Column 4 with appropriate modal verbs from Column 3. After filling in the blanks, explain the function of each modal verb to your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with appropriate modal verbs: (i) ____________ I leave early today if I finish all my tasks? [permission] (ii) I ____________ finish this report by tomorrow. [obligation] (iii) You ____________ consider all the pros and cons before making a decision. [advice] (iv) It ____________ rain later today. [possibility] (v) ____________ you pass the salt, please? [polite request] (vi) You ____________ apologise for the mistake you made. [moral obligation] (vii) We ____________ go to any park nearby for picnic. [suggestion]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Do you know how persons with visual challenges are able to read and write? They read through touch with the help of Braille. Study the Braille code given below and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

question_bank.stem: Study the Braille code and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Have you heard about the International Day of Persons with Disabilities? Find out when and how it is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.

question_bank.stem: Find out when and how the International Day of Persons with Disabilities is observed in India. Write your findings on a sheet and put it up on the class board.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Do you want to know about sign language? Go to the link given below to learn about the Indian sign language. https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf

question_bank.stem: Go to the link to learn about the Indian sign language: https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/accessibility/ISL_200word_v15.pdf
